args:
[{"id":"G-001","severity":"high","file":"src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py","line":"230-258","title":"Post-hoc direction selection (HARKing) for whole-corpus hypotheses locked with predicted_direction=None","description":"_resolve_direction accepts a confirm-time predicted_direction argument when the locked hypothesis has predicted_direction=None. A whole-corpus hypothesis can be locked/pre-registered with NO direction, then at confirm time the caller supplies the tail — choosing the direction AFTER seeing the data, which flips supported<->contradicted. This is the HARKing the pre-registration firewall exists to prevent. Unit-level has the same _resolve_direction but uses a caller flag; whole-corpus is the live concern.","poc":"Lock a WHOLE_CORPUS hypothesis with predicted_direction=None (passes lock_hypothesis since direction is optional). Call confirm_whole_corpus(h, observed=..., null=..., predicted_direction='greater') OR 'less' chosen after seeing which tail the observed statistic falls in — both are accepted, so the direction is selected post-hoc.","suggested_fix":"For WHOLE_CORPUS confirmation require predicted_direction to come from the LOCKED hypothesis (reject a confirm-time direction when the locked one is None), or require it be set at lock time. Mirror the pre-registration intent: the tail must be fixed before confirmation."},
{"id":"G-002","severity":"high","file":"src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py","line":"212","title":"Bootstrap lock_timeout session-GUC leaks into pooled connection reused for all later checkpoint ops","description":"_acquire_migration_lock runs SELECT set_config('lock_timeout', <ms>, false) on the bootstrap connection. is_local=false makes it a SESSION-level setting. That same connection returns to the pool and is reused for all later checkpoint reads/writes, which now silently run with a non-default lock_timeout — a surprising global side effect on every subsequent statement on that connection.","poc":"Bootstrap a checkpointer with ASSAY_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS=30000. The bootstrap connection gets lock_timeout=30s set session-wide, returns to the pool, and a later graph checkpoint write on that connection aborts after 30s instead of the server default (typically 0/unlimited) — a behavior change the operator never configured for normal ops.","suggested_fix":"Use set_config('lock_timeout', v, true) (local=true, transaction-scoped) inside an explicit transaction around the advisory lock + DDL, or RESET lock_timeout in the finally after the unlock, so the pooled connection returns with default settings."},
{"id":"G-003","severity":"high","file":"src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py","line":"253","title":"BULK tier has no inner cancellation (asymmetric with HIGH_STAKES) — a hung backend holds a pool slot the full outer timeout","description":"HIGH_STAKES wraps generation in an inner anyio.fail_after (pass-2 #127) so a hung call is cancelled promptly. BULK (_bulk_complete) has no equivalent inner deadline — it relies solely on the outer _with_timeout future.result(timeout). future.result timing out does NOT interrupt the running backend call, so a hung Ollama call holds its pool worker slot until the backend itself returns, even though the caller already got a timeout.","poc":"Point BULK at an Ollama endpoint that accepts the connection then never responds. _with_timeout raises FuturesTimeout to the caller after BULK_TIMEOUT, but the worker thread stays blocked in the backend call holding its _inflight slot; repeat until all 8 slots are stuck → RateLimitError storms despite the timeouts.","suggested_fix":"Give BULK the same inner bounded cancellation pattern as HIGH_STAKES (or a per-call HTTP client timeout on the Ollama client) so a hung call is actually abandoned and its slot freed, not just reported as timed out to the caller."},
{"id":"G-004","severity":"high","file":"src/assay_engine/_frozen.py","line":"146","title":"unfreeze() promises stable order for frozensets but falls back to nondeterministic set iteration on unorderable elements","description":"unfreeze sorts a frozenset for 'stable order for reproducible output' but on TypeError (mixed/unorderable element types) falls back to list(value) in iteration order, which is hash-seed/insertion dependent and NOT reproducible across processes. If such a structure is serialized into a provenance payload via to_records()/persist_trail(), two runs could emit different JSON orderings for the same logical content.","poc":"Put a frozenset of unorderable mixed types (e.g. {1, 'a'}) into a provenance payload, serialize via to_records() in two processes — the fallback list(value) order can differ, producing different serialized bytes for identical content (defeating reproducible output and any byte-equality check).","suggested_fix":"In the unorderable fallback, sort by a stable key (e.g. (type name, repr)) so the output order is deterministic regardless of hash seed/insertion order."},
{"id":"G-005","severity":"medium","file":"src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py","line":"333-337","title":"Contradicted verdict reported without any stability requirement, asymmetric with supported","description":"confirm_whole_corpus requires resample stability for a SUPPORTED verdict but returns CONTRADICTED (significant in the opposite tail) with no stability check at all. A single noisy null comparison in the opposite tail yields a hard 'contradicted' even if that contradiction would not reproduce across resamples — an asymmetric evidentiary bar that can over-state contradictions.","poc":"Provide observed significant in the opposite tail vs a null, with resample_statistics that do NOT stably reproduce that opposite-tail significance. confirm_whole_corpus returns CONTRADICTED immediately (before the stability gate), so an unstable contradiction is reported as decisively as a stable one.","suggested_fix":"Either require the same resample-stability standard for CONTRADICTED as for SUPPORTED, or document explicitly why contradiction is intentionally asymmetric (and record the missing stability in the verdict evidence as indeterminate-leaning)."},
{"id":"G-006","severity":"medium","file":"src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py","line":"39-53","title":"Direct confirm primitives default to presence-only pre-registration gate (authority=None)","description":"_gate_preregistration falls back to require_locked (cheap presence check) when authority=None, which is the default for confirm_whole_corpus/confirm_unit_level. A study calling the confirm primitives directly (outside the runners, which always pass an authority) gets only a presence check — a hand-set timestamp_proof string satisfies it without any cryptographic verification.","poc":"Construct a hypothesis with locked_at and a bogus timestamp_proof='x' (no real authority signature). Call confirm_whole_corpus(h, ..., authority=None) — it passes require_locked and confirms, despite the proof being unverifiable.","suggested_fix":"Make authority required for the public confirm primitives (no None default), or emit a warning when falling back to presence-only so a direct caller cannot silently skip real pre-registration verification."},
{"id":"G-007","severity":"medium","file":"scripts/capture_transcripts.py","line":"51","title":"Operator username PII survives transcript scrubbing outside /Users path contexts","description":"The pass-3 PII scrub redacts emails and the username only inside /Users/[REDACTED] or /home/[REDACTED] path segments. The bare operator username appearing elsewhere (e.g. in git author strings, prose, '[REDACTED]' as a GitHub handle, project dir slugs like -Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc) is NOT redacted, so the identity still leaks into committed transcripts.","poc":"A transcript line containing the Claude project dir slug '-Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc' or a git author '[REDACTED]' is not matched by the /Users|/home path regex, so the username remains in the committed transcript.","suggested_fix":"Add a standalone username token redaction (the operator's known handle) in addition to the path-context rule, or derive the handle from the home dir and redact it wherever it appears."},
{"id":"G-008","severity":"medium","file":"src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py","line":"85","title":"_CONN_INIT_LOCKS and _POOLS_BY_CONN grow unboundedly, keyed by conn_str, never pruned","description":"The per-conn_str lock map and pool cache are module-global dicts that only ever grow. A long-lived process that initializes checkpointers for many distinct conn_strs (e.g. multi-tenant, rotating credentials embedded in the URL, or test churn) accumulates Lock objects and ConnectionPool references forever — a slow memory leak and a growing set of live pools/worker threads.","poc":"Loop get_checkpointer() over many distinct conn_strs (vary dbname or embedded credentials). _POOLS_BY_CONN and _CONN_INIT_LOCKS grow without bound; each cached pool also keeps a worker thread alive.","suggested_fix":"Bound the caches (LRU with a sane cap, closing evicted pools) or document the single-conn_str assumption and assert/limit the number of distinct conn_strs a process will cache."},
{"id":"G-009","severity":"medium","file":"src/assay_engine/pipeline.py","line":"351","title":"run_study never persists or correlates the provenance trail to its MLflow run despite log_artifact existing","description":"run_study builds a hash-chained trail and exposes StudyResult.persist_trail (pass-3 F-045), but the pipeline itself never calls tracker.log_artifact to attach the serialized trail to the experiment run. The ExperimentTracker.log_artifact method exists and is unused by the runner, so the audit trail and the metrics run are not linked unless the caller manually persists+uploads.","poc":"Run a study with an MlflowExperimentTracker. The run has metrics but no provenance artifact; an auditor opening the MLflow run cannot retrieve the hash-chained trail that produced those metrics.","suggested_fix":"After verify(), best-effort serialize the trail to a temp file and tracker.log_artifact(run_id, path) (guard like the other tracker calls), so the trail is linked to the run when a tracker is present."},
{"id":"G-010","severity":"medium","file":"src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py","line":"217","title":"numpy resample-stability path allocates a full R×N boolean matrix (peak memory) instead of streaming per-resample","description":"The pass-3 F-017 numpy fast-path computes null_arr[None,:] >= res_arr[:,None] which materializes an R×N boolean array. For R=5000 resamples and N=50000 nulls that is 250M booleans (~250MB) held at once — the optimization trades the O(R*N) Python time for a large peak-memory allocation, which can OOM where the pure-Python loop would not.","poc":"Call _resample_stability with len(resamples)=5000 and len(null)=50000. The broadcast comparison allocates a ~250MB bool matrix in one shot, versus the pure-Python path's O(1) extra memory.","suggested_fix":"Chunk the resamples (process in batches) or use np.searchsorted on a sorted null (O((R+N) log N) time, O(R) memory) to get the same counts without the full R×N matrix."},
{"id":"G-011","severity":"medium","file":"src/assay_engine/methodology/discovery.py","line":"150","title":"discover_and_confirm does not type-guard the confirmer's return value, asymmetric with the pipeline","description":"Pass-3 F-021 added isinstance(verdict, Verdict) guards in the pipeline and adjudication paths, but the standalone discover_and_confirm runner accesses verdict.hypothesis_id without checking the confirmer returned a Verdict. A confirmer that returns None there raises a raw AttributeError instead of FirewallViolation — the same inconsistency F-021 fixed elsewhere.","poc":"Call discover_and_confirm(..., confirm=lambda h, c: None). At the verdict.hypothesis_id access it raises AttributeError: 'NoneType' has no attribute 'hypothesis_id', not the typed FirewallViolation the pipeline path raises.","suggested_fix":"Add the same isinstance(verdict, Verdict) guard in discover_and_confirm before reading verdict.hypothesis_id, raising FirewallViolation to match the pipeline/adjudication contract."},
{"id":"G-012","severity":"medium","file":"src/assay_engine/provenance.py","line":"266","title":"from_records raises raw KeyError on a malformed record instead of typed ProvenanceError","description":"from_records indexes record fields (e.g. r['seq'], r['kind'], r['entry_hash']) directly. A record dict missing a key raises a bare KeyError rather than the typed ProvenanceError the module uses for chain-integrity failures, so a caller catching ProvenanceError to handle a corrupt/incompatible trail file will miss this case.","poc":"from_records([{ 'seq':0, 'kind':'x' }]) (missing summary/payload/hashes) raises KeyError('summary'), not ProvenanceError — inconsistent with verify_records which raises ProvenanceError on integrity problems.","suggested_fix":"Validate required keys and raise ProvenanceError (with the missing field) for a malformed record, so all trail-deserialization failures surface as the one typed error."},
{"id":"G-013","severity":"medium","file":"tests/test_baseline.py","line":"237","title":"test for cosine_similarity_matrix numpy fast path passes even if the fast path is removed (non-discriminating)","description":"The test asserts only the numeric result of cosine_similarity_matrix, which the pure-Python fallback computes identically. If the numpy fast path were deleted/broken, the test still passes — it does not actually guard that the fast path is used or correct, so a regression in the optimized branch is invisible.","poc":"Delete or break the numpy branch in cosine_similarity_matrix; the test still passes because the fallback yields the same numbers. The test name implies it guards the fast path but it does not.","suggested_fix":"Make the test discriminate the fast path (e.g. assert equivalence between forced-numpy and forced-pure paths, or monkeypatch to assert the numpy branch is taken), or rename it to reflect it only checks numeric correctness."},
{"id":"G-014","severity":"medium","file":"tests/test_observability.py","line":"162","title":"test_install_flush_on_exit_wires_atexit_and_sigterm passes even if SIGTERM wiring is removed","description":"The test guards the atexit flush but the SIGTERM handler assertion is conditional ('if \"h\" in captured') so if the SIGTERM wiring is removed/never installed the branch is simply skipped and the test still passes. The SIGTERM flush-on-stop behavior (important for container stop) is therefore not actually guarded.","poc":"Remove the signal.signal(SIGTERM, ...) wiring in _install_flush_on_exit. The test's 'if h in captured' guard is False, the SIGTERM assertions are skipped, and the test passes — the regression is invisible.","suggested_fix":"On the main thread, assert the SIGTERM handler WAS installed and that invoking it flushes+shuts down (unconditionally, not gated on 'if h in captured'); skip only when genuinely not on the main thread."},
{"id":"G-015","severity":"medium","file":".github/workflows/ci.yml","line":"135","title":"SBOM step re-runs pip-audit WITHOUT the allowlist, so an accepted advisory fails the SBOM gate","description":"The pip-audit gate honors .pip-audit-ignore, but the separate SBOM step runs pip-audit (cyclonedx) without the --ignore-vuln allowlist and with set -euo pipefail (no || true after F-012). An advisory that is accepted/allowlisted in the gate will cause the SBOM pip-audit invocation to exit non-zero and fail CI — making the allowlist ineffective and coupling SBOM generation to vuln status.","poc":"Add a real advisory id to .pip-audit-ignore (gate passes). The SBOM step's pip-audit (no allowlist) still detects the vuln, exits non-zero under pipefail, and fails the audit job despite the advisory being formally accepted.","suggested_fix":"Generate the SBOM with a tool that doesn't gate on vulns (e.g. cyclonedx-py/cyclonedx-bom from the lockfile), or pass the same allowlist, or use --output without failing on findings, so SBOM emission is decoupled from the vuln gate."},
{"id":"G-016","severity":"medium","file":".github/dependabot.yml","line":"8","title":"Dependabot bumps pyproject ranges but cannot regenerate requirements.lock, so the sync gate blocks every bump","description":"Dependabot is configured for pip (pyproject) but the repo also commits a hash-pinned requirements.lock enforced by the CI sync check. Dependabot does not know how to regenerate that uv lockfile, so any Dependabot PR that changes pyproject will fail the 'requirements.lock is stale' gate, making every automated bump un-mergeable without manual lock regeneration.","poc":"Let Dependabot open a PR bumping a dependency in pyproject.toml. The audit job's lockfile-sync diff fails because requirements.lock/requirements-core.lock were not regenerated, blocking the PR.","suggested_fix":"Add a CI workflow that regenerates+commits the lockfiles on Dependabot PRs (uv pip compile), or document the manual regeneration step, or switch the lock to a format Dependabot maintains."},
{"id":"G-017","severity":"low","file":"src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py","line":"162-168","title":"Unit-level confirm validates predicted_direction shape but never uses it (dead validation / latent confusion)","description":"Pass-3 F-002 added a defensive predicted_direction check in confirm_unit_level, but the unit-level path decides direction purely from the caller's direction_supports_claim flag and never consults predicted_direction. The validation rejects bad values but the field is otherwise inert at unit level, which can mislead a reader into thinking the locked direction governs the unit-level verdict (it does not).","poc":"A UNIT_LEVEL hypothesis with predicted_direction='greater' but a caller-supplied direction_supports_claim=False yields CONTRADICTED regardless of the locked 'greater' — the locked direction has no effect, only the flag does.","suggested_fix":"Either cross-check direction_supports_claim against the locked predicted_direction (when present) and reject a mismatch, or document clearly that unit-level direction is governed solely by the caller flag and the locked direction is advisory."},
{"id":"G-018","severity":"low","file":"src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py","line":"136","title":"scrubbed_env omits some off-box redirect env vars the Claude CLI/SDK honors","description":"is_unsafe_subprocess_var covers ANTHROPIC_*, AWS_*, GOOGLE_*, *_PROXY and an explicit set, but may omit other egress/redirect vars the CLI/SDK reads (e.g. ANTHROPIC_CUSTOM_HEADERS, HTTP(S)_PROXY variants already covered, but also things like CLAUDE_CODE_* config beyond the listed ones, or NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS / proxy bypass). Incomplete denylist risks an off-box redirect slipping through.","poc":"Set an env var the CLI honors for endpoint/header override that is not in the denylist (e.g. a custom-headers or extra-CA var) — scrubbed_env passes it through to the high-stakes subprocess.","suggested_fix":"Switch the high-stakes subprocess to an ALLOWLIST (pass only the minimal known-safe vars: PATH, HOME, the OAuth token, locale) instead of a denylist, so unknown redirect vars are dropped by default."},
{"id":"G-019","severity":"low","file":"src/assay_engine/orchestration/gates.py","line":"88","title":"Gate.evaluate reads decision.approved without confirming the handler returned a GateDecision","description":"Gate evaluation accesses decision attributes (approved/reason) via getattr with defaults in run_gate, but the orchestration Gate.evaluate path may access decision.approved directly. A handler returning a non-GateDecision could raise AttributeError instead of a typed gate error — the F-021 class applied to gate handlers.","poc":"A gate handler returning None or a dict instead of a GateDecision triggers an AttributeError on attribute access rather than a clear typed error explaining the handler contract violation.","suggested_fix":"Type-guard the handler return (isinstance GateDecision) and raise a clear GateError naming the contract, consistent with the pass-3 Verdict guards."},
{"id":"G-020","severity":"low","file":"src/assay_engine/orchestration/gatenode.py","line":"145","title":"make_gate_node re-raises a recorder failure after the interrupt resume was already consumed","description":"In the gate node, recorder(rec) runs after apply_gate_decision on a valid resume. If the recorder (provenance trail write) raises, it propagates out of the node AFTER the interrupt resume value was already consumed/persisted, so a re-run replays from the next interrupt index without re-recording — the decision is applied to graph state but missing from the hash-chained trail, a silent audit gap on recorder failure.","poc":"Supply a recorder that raises on the first call. The resume is consumed, the node raises, and on resume the decision exists in graph state but the trail has no gate entry for it.","suggested_fix":"Record to the trail BEFORE returning the state update (and make the trail write part of the same failure-atomic step), or catch+surface recorder failure as a typed error that halts rather than silently diverging graph state from the trail."},
{"id":"G-021","severity":"low","file":"src/assay_engine/baseline/determinism.py","line":"140","title":"build_baseline_artifact lets hash_value raise raw ValueError/TypeError on non-canonicalizable extra_inputs","description":"build_baseline_artifact hashes extra_inputs via hash_value, which raises raw ValueError (non-finite) / TypeError (unhashable) without context about WHICH extra input failed. A study passing a rich config with an unserializable value gets an opaque error rather than one naming the offending key.","poc":"build_baseline_artifact(corpus, {}, extra_inputs={'cfg': object()}) raises a raw TypeError from hash_value with no indication that 'cfg' is the bad key.","suggested_fix":"Wrap the per-extra-input hashing to catch and re-raise with the key name (e.g. 'extra_input cfg is not canonicalizable: ...'), so the error is actionable."},
{"id":"G-022","severity":"low","file":".github/workflows/ci.yml","line":"97","title":"Audit-gate tooling (pip-audit, pip-licenses, cyclonedx) installed unpinned, undermining reproducible supply-chain scans","description":"The audit job installs pip-audit and pip-licenses with no version pin (pip install pip-audit / pip-licenses). A new tool release can change advisory results, output schema, or license classification, so the supply-chain gate is not reproducible run-to-run — the same commit can pass today and fail tomorrow on a tooling change, the analogue of the uv-pin fix (F-031).","poc":"A new pip-audit release adds an advisory or changes exit behavior; a previously-green commit now fails the audit job with no repo change — non-reproducible gate.","suggested_fix":"Pin pip-audit, pip-licenses (and any cyclonedx tool) to exact versions, consistent with the uv==0.11.21 pin from F-031."},
{"id":"G-023","severity":"low","file":"src/assay_engine/pipeline.py","line":"445","title":"Pipeline builds the corpus unit-id set twice on the same corpus (redundant O(U))","description":"run_study constructs {u.unit_id for u in corpus.units} in more than one place (e.g. the split-id validation and the feature-builder ghost-id check), recomputing the same set on the same corpus. Minor redundant O(U) work on large corpora; trivially hoistable.","poc":"Inspect run_study: the unit-id set comprehension appears for split validation and again for feature ghost-id validation on the same corpus.units, recomputed rather than reused.","suggested_fix":"Compute unit_ids once after ingest and reuse it for both the split-id and feature-id validations."},
{"id":"G-024","severity":"low","file":"src/assay_engine/baseline/primitives.py","line":"125","title":"Comment claims determinism freeze keeps an array opaque/O(1) but freeze() copies tobytes() (doc inaccuracy)","description":"A comment near the determinism/primitives array handling claims the array is kept opaque or O(1), but freeze() actually materializes tobytes() into a (kind, shape, bytes) tuple — an O(size) copy. The comment understates the cost, the assertion-vs-implementation class from this pass.","poc":"Read the comment vs freeze() in _frozen.py: freeze copies the full buffer via tobytes(), contradicting any 'opaque/O(1)' characterization in the primitives comment.","suggested_fix":"Correct the comment to state the array buffer is copied (O(size)) into a hashable descriptor, not kept opaque/O(1)."}]
error:
TypeError: pipeline() expects an array as the first argument
    at <anonymous> (/$bunfs/root/src/entrypoints/cli.js:3702:6923)
    at processTicksAndRejections (native:7:39)
